title: Mapo Tofu

ingredients.name: firm tofu, cut into 2 cm cubes

subs.name: 200 g paneer, cubed, for an Indian-friendly version
omit entirely and add 2 extra tbsp chilli-bean paste for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: reduce oil by 1 tbsp if skipping meat

ingredients.name: chilli bean paste (doubanjiang) or chilli-garlic sauce
visual_checks: doubanjiang is traditional; sambal oelek or chilli-garlic paste are good substitutes
ingredients.notes: doubanjiang is traditional; sambal oelek or chilli-garlic paste are good substitutes

subs.name: 2 tbsp red chilli paste or extra red chilli powder + 1 tsp vinegar
subs.notes: the fermented, savoury note will be missing but the heat will be there

ingredients.name: Sichuan peppercorns, toasted and ground (or black pepper)
visual_checks: Sichuan peppercorns give the signature mala numbing heat; black pepper is a milder substitute
ingredients.notes: Sichuan peppercorns give the signature mala numbing heat; black pepper is a milder substitute

ingredients.name: cornflour mixed with 3 tbsp cold water

subs.name: water with a stock cube
subs.notes: add a little soy sauce to compensate

steps.text: Bring a pot of lightly salted water to a boil. Gently slide in the tofu cubes and blanch for 2 minutes — this firms them up so they hold their shape in the sauce. Drain carefully and set aside.
Heat oil in a wok or kadai over medium-high heat. Add the minced meat and stir-fry, breaking it up, until it loses its pink colour and begins to brown at the edges, about 3 minutes.

Push the meat to one side and add the chilli-bean paste to the exposed oil. Fry the paste for 1 minute, stirring constantly, until it darkens slightly and the oil turns deep red. Mix together with the meat.
Add garlic and ginger; stir-fry for 1 minute. Add soy sauce, red chilli powder and ground Sichuan pepper. Stir to combine.

Pour in the stock and bring to a simmer. Gently lower the blanched tofu cubes into the sauce — do not stir aggressively or they will break. Simmer on medium heat for 5 minutes, spooning sauce over the tofu occasionally.
Stir the cornflour slurry and pour it in gradually, gently rocking the wok to incorporate. The sauce should thicken to coat the back of a spoon within 1 minute. Add salt to taste.
Scatter the spring onions over the top and serve immediately with steamed rice. The sauce is meant to be thin enough to mix into the rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tofu → 200 g paneer, cubed, for an Indian-friendly version (paneer does not need blanching); keema → omit entirely and add 2 extra tbsp chilli-bean paste for a vegetarian version (reduce oil by 1 tbsp if skipping meat); green-chilli-sauce → 2 tbsp red chilli paste or extra red chilli powder + 1 tsp vinegar (the fermented, savoury note will be missing but the heat will be there); stock → water with a stock cube (add a little soy sauce to compensate)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Firm tofu (sold as 'bean curd' in Indian supermarkets and Chinese grocery shops) works beautifully here; paneer is a reliable backup if tofu is unavailable. Use a chilli-garlic sauce (Chings brand is widely stocked) in place of doubanjiang. Cook in a kadai on medium-high flame. Sichuan peppercorns can sometimes be found in specialty spice shops in metros; otherwise use ½ tsp black pepper and ½ tsp garam masala for a faint numbing warmth.
